fact: March 1 is exactly 60 days into the year - that's like two whole months of Earth spinning around!
In the Northern Hemisphere, March 1 sits right in the sweet spot where winter is throwing its last tantrum but spring is already sneaking in through the cracks.
Maple trees in cold regions start getting their sap flowing around this time, which means pancake syrup season is beginning!
The sun rises about 2 minutes earlier each day around March 1 in most places - that's like gaining a whole extra hour of morning light every month!
March 1 marks the meteorological start of spring in the Northern Hemisphere, which means weather scientists officially consider winter over even if it doesn't feel like it.
Earthworms start wiggling closer to the surface around this date because the ground begins thawing, making them easier targets for hungry robins.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere places, March 1 is when autumn officially kicks in and leaves start their colorful death dance.
Daylight on March 1 lasts almost exactly 11 hours in most temperate regions - we're racing toward that perfect 12-hour balance of the spring equinox.
Hibernating animals like bears typically start stirring in their dens around this date because their internal clocks sense the changing light, even underground.
March 1 sits just 20 days before the spring equinox, when day and night become perfectly equal - like nature's way of finding perfect balance.
